EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking just south of the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to portions of Downeast Maine. The snow began during the evening of the 5th and continued into the morning of the 6th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 9 inches...with the heaviest snow along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent trof focused an extended interval of heavy snow across northern and portions of eastern Maine. Snow began during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the evening of the 23rd. The heaviest snow fell across northern and northwestern portions of Aroostook county where storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 15 inches were common...with 16 to 20 inches across the Saint John Valley where isolated totals exceeded 20 inches. A sharp gradient of diminishing snow totals occurred south of the trof.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a well outside a home in North Yarmouth and is believed to have followed underground wiring to the home where it ignited a fire in the basement.  The fire then completely destroyed the home.  Six people were at the home at the time and all got out safely, but one of the family's dogs perished in the blaze.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 14 to 20 inches...with locally up to 22 inches reported in the vicinity of Fort Kent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure moving through the Gulf of Maine coincided with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce coastal flooding in areas along the Maine coast. Strong southeast winds produced a storm surge of 1.44 ft and near shore waves of 7 to 10 ft, bringing the Portland tide gage to 12.54 ft. Ocean water flooded Route 1 in Lincolnville and Frankfort. Water reached cars parked near Commercial Street in Portland. In Kennebunk powerful waves crashed in and around homes. At Popham Beach, trees were sucked into the ocean and severe beach erosion occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure and astronomically high tides produced coastal flooding in the Lincolville/Frankfort area on Route 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the Carolina coast on the morning of the 5th and moved northeast to south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 6th.  The low brought 4 to 8 inches of snow to coastal and interior areas.  The foothills generally received from 2 to 6 inches with only an inch or two in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the Carolina coast on the morning of the 5th and moved northeast to south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 6th.  The low brought 4 to 8 inches of snow to coastal and interior areas.  The foothills generally received from 2 to 6 inches with only an inch or two in the mountains.

An area of low pressure over the southeastern states on the morning of the 1st moved northeast along the coast to just east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 2nd and then into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 3rd.  The storm brought heavy snow to most of western Maine, except in the mountains where lighter amounts fell.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in the western Maine mountains to 6 to 10 inches along the coast and interior.

An area of low pressure in the mid-Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 21st moved northeast through the Great Lakes by the morning of the 22nd.  A secondary low developed off the mid-Atlantic coast during the day on the 22nd, and then moved northeast along the coast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 23rd.  The combination of the two systems brought heavy snow to most of western Maine.  In extreme coastal York County, much of the precipitation fell as rain.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about 2 inches in the Kittery area to 1 to 2 feet in the interior, foothills, and mountains.

A large area of low pressure moved northeast through the Great Lakes and into southern Canada on the 9th.  A secondary low developed off the East Coast and moved rapidly northeast.  The low brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across the state.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across most of the state with somewhat lesser amounts in coastal areas.  The snow changed to rain before ending.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds downing large tree limbs near Harrison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm downed large limbs on Summit Hill Road near Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire that severely damaged a home on the Isle au Haut.  The house was in the process of being remodeled and was just weeks away from being complete.   The small island community turned out in force to fight the fire which also threatened a 100-year-old island school house nearby.

Lightning ignited a fire in the basement of a home in Freeport causing an estimated $50,000 in damage.  The lightning struck a nearby tree and then travelled through the ground and into the cellar of the home.  The fire was contained to the basement but the rest of the house had smoke damage.

Two teenage baseball players were struck by lightning, but suffered only minor injuries.  The two (along with the rest of the team) had taken shelter in a dugout when the storm approached.  One of the boys was leaning against an electrical outlet when the lightning struck.  The current passed through the two boys and into the ground.
Low pressure moving through New England produced around 3 inches of rain over southwest Maine. Minor flooding resulted on the Presumpscot River in Westbrook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook, (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 15.7 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms continued to form over the same area of Somerset County near Bingham resulting in 3 to 5 inches of rainfall in 3 hours. Numerous washouts were reported on Mahoney Hill Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloping over the same general area just east of Bingham resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rain in 3 hours. This caused numerous washouts on Mahoney Hill Road which was closed by the state police.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak cold front and short wave approaching from the Great Lakes. Heating in the morning provided a marginally unstable environment. Shear was expected to be relatively weak, and low wet bulb zero values suggested marginally severe hail was possible. Several severe thunderstorms did produce .75 hail along with one report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees taking down power lines and destroying one house in Bridgton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a shortwave approaching from the west. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail in York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail near Springvale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of heavy rain developed in advance of a warm front resulting in 2 to 3 inches of rain in 90 minutes in York County. This caused some road washouts in Arundel and central York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding and some road washouts in Arundel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rain developed in advance of a warm front resulting in 3 to 5 inches of rain in 90 minutes. This caused flash flooding of small creeks and streams. Many roads in the 3 counties of Knox, Lincoln, and Waldo were closed because of severe road damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rain in 90 minutes causing flash flooding in Knox County. Many roads were damaged or washed out completely in the towns of Union, Appleton, and Washington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rain in 90 minutes causing flash flooding in Lincoln County. Many roads were damaged in the towns of Edgecomb, Jefferson, Newcastle, and Waldoboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed across Washington County during the evening of the 30th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The thunderstorm produced large hail.

An area of low pressure located over the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 10th moved east to off the New England coast by the morning of the 11th.  The low brought from 4 to 8 inches of snow to York County with generally 3 to 6 inches across the remainder of western Maine.

An isolated severe thunderstorm developed across eastern Aroostook county during the evening of the 6th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail continued for around 7 minutes...leaving dents in some vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon of the 26th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The thunderstorms continued into the evening. The thunderstorms produced a weak tornado...large hail...damaging winds and flash flooding.

Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon of the 26th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The thunderstorms continued into the evening. The thunderstorms produced a weak tornado...large hail...damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms containing very heavy rain produced localized rainfall totals of up to 3.00 to 4.50 inches in 1 to 2 hours. These rains produced flash flooding with 5 sections of Route 2 flooded between the towns of Sherman and Island Falls. A one mile portion of the road had water depths of 12 inches or more covering the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced an EF0 tornado just east and southeast of the town of Stockholm...in the vicinity of School Street. The tornado sporadically reached tree top level along its path...shearing the tops off some trees (DI 27, DOD 2). Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated at around 65 mph. The average path width was around 25 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a century-old home in Rumford and caused a fire that destroyed the residence.  Damage was estimated at about $200,000.

Intensifying low pressure tracking along the Maine coast to New Brunswick brought heavy snow to northern and eastern Maine. Strong winds developed along the Downeast coast in advance of the low. Snow developed across the region through the morning and early afternoon hours of the 9th...continuing through the afternoon. The snow then ended...from south to north...through the evening of the 9th into the early morning hours of the 10th. Storm total snow accumulations across much of northern and eastern Maine ranged from 5 to 8 inches. Snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were common across the west-central highlands...where localized totals of up to 12 inches were reported. The snow briefly transitioned to a wintry mix across interior Downeast areas. Enough warm air reached the Downeast coast to allow the snow to eventually transition to all rain. The combination of heavy wet snow on trees and power lines along with the strong winds led to trees and power lines being toppled and power outages along the Downeast coast. The greatest impact was across Hancock county where nearly 4000 custromers lost power at various times...with the most significant outages in the Stonington and Deer Isle areas. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common along the Downeast coast...with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at West Quoddy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered convection developed along a slow moving cold front in the early afternoon of August 5th. Despite dew points near 70 south of the front, convection was widely scattered due to a mid level cap. The storms that broke the cap took advantage of moderate instability and 30 knots of bulk shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Windsor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and branches on power lines causing several small fires in Northport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Northport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced estimated wind gusts of 60 mph over the waters 2 miles east of Lincolnville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure moving through the Gulf of Maine coincided with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce coastal flooding in areas along the Maine coast. Strong southeast winds produced a storm surge of 1.44 ft and near shore waves of 7 to 10 ft, bringing the Portland tide gage to 12.54 ft. Ocean water flooded Route 1 in Lincolnville and Frankfort. Water reached cars parked near Commercial Street in Portland. In Kennebunk powerful waves crashed in and around homes. At Popham Beach, trees were sucked into the ocean and severe beach erosion occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure and astronomically high tides produced coastal flooding in Kennebunk with powerful waves crashing in and around ocean front homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida produced heavy rain across New Hampshire and Maine. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were common with Wells, Maine reporting 6.30 inches. The rain produced minor flooding on the Presumpscot River in Westbrook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from the remnants of Hurricane Ida caused minor flooding on the Presumpscot River in Westbrook, (flood stage 15.0 ft).  The river crested at 18.76 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida slowly meandered off the coast before weakening and moving out to sea on the 16th. High storm tides, (10.8 ft in Portland Harbor), combined with large waves to produce splash-over around the time of high tide. York Beach had minor erosion of road shoulders of beach roads. Wehannett Street in Wells reported minor damage as did the town of Kennebunk and South Portland. Streets flooded with water and small rocks on Surf and Riverside Streets in Saco.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida produced large waves which resulted in minor beach erosion and splash-over in Saco at the time of high tide.

The combination of heavy wet snow on trees and power lines along with the strong winds led to trees and power lines being toppled and power outages along the Downeast coast. The greatest impact was across Hancock county where nearly 4000 custromers lost power at various times...with the most significant outages in the Stonington and Deer Isle areas. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common along the Downeast coast...with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at West Quoddy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent trof focused an extended interval of heavy snow across northern and portions of eastern Maine. Snow began during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the evening of the 23rd. The heaviest snow fell across northern and northwestern portions of Aroostook county where storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 15 inches were common...with 16 to 20 inches across the Saint John Valley where isolated totals exceeded 20 inches. A sharp gradient of diminishing snow totals occurred south of the trof.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 15 inches.

Storm total snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches occurred in a small area from central to southern Penobscot county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds developed across Downeast areas in advance of low pressure crossing Maine. The winds toppled trees and power lines along the Downeast coast where approximately 1500 customers lost power at various times during the event. The peak wind gust during the event was 63 mph reported in the town of Hancock. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common along the coast. The strong onshore winds...combined with higher than normal tides....produced coastal flooding along portions of the Downeast coast. The strong winds through Penobscot Bay forced water to funnel back up into the Penobscot River...which in turn then backed up into the Kenduskeag Stream which led to minor flooding in downtown Bangor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common from the late morning through the afternoon hours of the 3rd. A peak wind gust of 63 mph was reported at the town of Hancock. The strong winds toppled trees and power lines contributing to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving through the Ohio Valley and a secondary low developing off the southern New England coast combined to produce heavy snow across the western half of the state, with a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in southern areas.  Along the immediate coast, the mixed precipitation changed to rain for a brief period. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches along the coast to 10 to 14 inches inland.

Arctic air overspread all of northern and eastern Maine during the 14th and remained across the region into the 18th. During this time overnight low temperatures of 35 to 45 degrees below zero were common across northern Maine...with local temperatues of 45 to 50 below zero. The temperature at Big Black River fell to 50 degrees below zero during the morning of the 16th...establishing a new all-time record low for the state of Maine. Temperatures never rose above zero across portions of northern Maine from the 14th into the 18th. Across Downeast areas...overnight low temperatures of 15 to 25 degrees below zero were common with local temperatures of 30 to 35 below. Several daily record low temperatures were also established across the region from the 14th into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic air overspread the area during the 14th and remained into the 18th.

An area of low pressure tracking east from the Great Lakes brought heavy snow to the mountains of Western Maine.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 5 to 9 inches across the region.

Arctic air overspread all of northern and eastern Maine during the 14th and remained across the region into the 18th. During this time overnight low temperatures of 35 to 45 degrees below zero were common across northern Maine...with local temperatues of 45 to 50 below zero. The temperature at Big Black River fell to 50 degrees below zero during the morning of the 16th...establishing a new all-time record low for the state of Maine. Temperatures never rose above zero across portions of northern Maine from the 14th into the 18th. Across Downeast areas...overnight low temperatures of 15 to 25 degrees below zero were common with local temperatures of 30 to 35 below. Several daily record low temperatures were also established across the region from the 14th into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic air overspread the area during the 14th and remained into the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain along with melting snow combined to produce minor flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt to produce minor flooding on the Presumpscot River which crested at 15.04 ft (flood stage 15.0 ft).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front associated with low pressure over the Great Lakes approached the region from the southwest on the morning of December 9th. A secondary area of low pressure developed on the front in the afternoon and tracked into the Gulf of Maine. High winds associated with this system produced a wind gust of 61 mph measured at the Pemequid Point Light House in South Bristol.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through the Gulf of Maine produced a measured wind gust of 61 mph at the Pemequid Light House in South Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking along the Maine coast brought heavy snow to all of northern and eastern Maine. Snow began during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 28th then continued into the early morning hours of the 29th. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times across Downeast areas...with 2/10 to 3/10 of an inch of ice accumulation in spots along with the snow. Storm total snow accumulations across northern Maine generally ranged from 9 to 14 inches...with 6 to 10 inches across central and interior Downeast areas and 5 to 8 inches along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking northeast across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to most of northern and eastern Maine. Bands of heavy snow developed across central portions of the region from the late evening of the 22nd through the early morning hours of the 23rd. Snow fell at the rate of 2 to 4 inches per hour at times within these bands. Storm total snowfall across northeast Maine generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches. The greatest storm totals occurred across central portions of the region where the heavy snow bands developed producing generally 18 to 24 inches of accumulation...with local totals approaching 30 inches. Across interior Downeast areas storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches. Along the Downeast coast storm total snow generally ranged from 9 to 16 inches...where the precipitation transitioned to rain for a time.|Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph occurred across much of the region and combined with the snow to produce considerable blowing and drifting snow with near whiteout conditions at times. Along the Downeast coast winds briefly gusted to 55 to 65 mph just in advance of the low. The heavy wet snow and strong winds combined to snap branches and bring down power lines. This produced extensive power outages...particularly along the Downeast coast. Around 12000 customers lost power in Hancock county...with around 3000 customers loosing power in Washington county. The greatest impact in both counties was along the coast. Power outages across the entire state were significant enough that the Governor declared a State of Emergency in order to facilitate power restoration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 15 to 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to northern and northwest Maine. The snow began during the afternoon of the 7th...then continued through the early morning hours of the 8th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 11 inches. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times...with ice accumulations 1/10 of an inch or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 11 inches. Localized ice accumulations were 1/10 of an inch or less.

Cold air wrapping around low pressure crossing the Gulf of Maine allowed rain to transition to snow across northern Aroostook county. The transition to snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th...with snow then continuing through the afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches...with local totals of 10 to 12 inches across extreme northern portions of Aroostook county. The greatest snow accumulations occurred across the Saint John River Valley and higher elevation areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking through the Gulf of Maine to the Gulf of Saint Lawrence brought heavy snow and mixed precipitation to east-central and Downeast Maine. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 2nd...with precipitation then continuing through the late afternoon and evening hours. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain across Hancock and Washington counties. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches across much of the region...with 3 to 6 inches along the Downeast coast. Additionally...across Hancock and Washington counties...sleet accumulated 1 to 3 inches along with up to 1/4 of an inch of ice from freezing rain. The combination of heavy wet snow and ice accumulation on power lines...along with gusty winds...contributed to power outages across Washington county. Around 2000 customers lost power in Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

The combination of heavy wet snow and ice accumulation on power lines...along with gusty winds...contributed to power outages across Washington county. Around 2000 customers lost power in Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking northeast across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to most of northern and eastern Maine. Bands of heavy snow developed across central portions of the region from the late evening of the 22nd through the early morning hours of the 23rd. Snow fell at the rate of 2 to 4 inches per hour at times within these bands. Storm total snowfall across northeast Maine generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches. The greatest storm totals occurred across central portions of the region where the heavy snow bands developed producing generally 18 to 24 inches of accumulation...with local totals approaching 30 inches. Across interior Downeast areas storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches. Along the Downeast coast storm total snow generally ranged from 9 to 16 inches...where the precipitation transitioned to rain for a time.|Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph occurred across much of the region and combined with the snow to produce considerable blowing and drifting snow with near whiteout conditions at times. Along the Downeast coast winds briefly gusted to 55 to 65 mph just in advance of the low. The heavy wet snow and strong winds combined to snap branches and bring down power lines. This produced extensive power outages...particularly along the Downeast coast. Around 12000 customers lost power in Hancock county...with around 3000 customers loosing power in Washington county. The greatest impact in both counties was along the coast. Power outages across the entire state were significant enough that the Governor declared a State of Emergency in order to facilitate power restoration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 15 inches. The combination of heavy wet snow and strong gusty winds snapped branches and brought down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest onshore winds developed during the evening of the 27th in advance of a cold front crossing the region...with the strong winds continuing into the early morning hours of the 28th. Estimated wind gusts were up to 60 mph. The strong winds exacerbated damage from the storm which had occurred less than a week earlier...resulting in over 4000 customer loosing power again due to downed trees and power lines. The greatest impact was in the town of Somesville on Mount Desert Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore south to southwest winds...with estimated gusts up to 60 mph...toppled trees and power lines contributing to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the mid-Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 18th to southern Ontario by the morning of the 19th.  A secondary low developed in the Gulf of Maine during the day on the 19th and moved to New Brunswick by the morning of the 20th.  The combination of low pressure systems brought heavy snow to most of the western part of the state with 6 to 10 inches of accumulation being reported.

A combination of rain and run-off from melting snow led to prolonged minor lowland flooding of the Mattawamkeag River at Mattawamkeag. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas bordering the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and run-off from melting snow led to a prolonged interval of minor flooding on the Mattawamkeag River at Mattawamkeag. The river slowly rose and crested around 1.5 feet above flood stage. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas bordering the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River at Masardis contributed to flooding in the Masardis area. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas along the river. However...the flooding did lead to the closure of Route 11. Several homes were also affected. River levels receded with the release of the ice jam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River at Masardis contributed to flooding in the Masardis area. The river crested around 1.5 feet above flood stage. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas along the river. However...the flooding did lead to the closure of Route 11. Several homes were also affected by the flooding. One home had water filling the basement...while a second home had around 2 feet of water on the first floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and run-off from melting snow led to minor flooding along the Big Black River at Depot Mountain. The flooding affected wilderness areas of the North Maine Woods bordering the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and run-off from melting snow led to minor flooding along the Big Black River at Depot Mountain. The river crested around 1 foot above flood stage. The flooding affected wilderness areas of the North Maine Woods bordering the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Ohio River Valley and into New England spreading heavy rain over the region. Rainfall amounts and rainfall rates where high enough to cause serious flooding in midcoast Maine. Rainfall amounts in the damaged areas ranged from 3 to 4.5 inches in 12 hours. Damage to roads in the area was extensive with blown out culverts and washed out roadbeds. Some schools in Appleton, Camden, Hope, and Licolnville were closed because of the road closures. Damage in Knox, Waldo, and Lincoln Counties amounted to more than $850,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused serious flooding in several midcoast Maine towns on the 6th through the 8th of April. Small rivers and streams overflowed washing out roads and culverts. Damage estimates for Waldo county were $618,968. Some of the hardest hit towns included Belfast ($100,000), Northport ($150,000), Frankfort ($76,000), and Searsport ($60,000).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the Ohio River Valley resulting in .75 to 1 inch of rain. This rain combined with snowmelt caused minor flooding on the Kennebec river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt caused a brief period on minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan,(flood stage 35,000 cfs) which crested at 35,600 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of snowmelt and 1 to 1.5 inches of rain caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at North Sidney, Augusta, and Skowhegan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt resulted in minor flooding on the Kennebec River at North Sidney (flood stage 17.0 ft.) which crested at 17.8 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of snowmelt and 1 to 1.5 inches of rain caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at North Sidney, Augusta, and Skowhegan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta (flood stage 12.0 ft.) which crested at 14.4 ft.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt resulted in minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs) which crested at 43488 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving out of eastern New York into northern New England produced a moderate to strong mid level jet and good height falls over moderately unstable lower layers. Good speed and some directional shear resulted in rotating cells which evolved into bows and lines. Some large hail was reported in New Hampshire, but only wind damage was reported in Maine from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm downed trees on wires in Baldwin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late afternoon and evening of the 31st in advance of a cold front and upper disturbance crossing the region. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...large hail and several tornados.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 inch diameter spruce tree was toppled on State Route 11 by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late afternoon and evening of the 31st in advance of a cold front and upper disturbance crossing the region. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...large hail and several tornados.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado near the town of Westfield. The tornado began near the Little Burnt Land Stream area...then was intermittently on the ground before lifting just west of U.S. Route 1 near Westfield. Along the tornado path...hundreds of trees were snapped and uprooted in intermittent swaths and patches (DI 27, DOD 4). Several outbuildings were also damaged or destroyed along Simpson and Shorey Roads near Westfield. Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated at around 95 mph. This same thunderstorm continued tracking northeast across U.S. Route 1 and Westfield...producing another tornado several miles further along its track in the vicinity of Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in advance of a cold front crossing the region...with wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph common. The strong winds developed during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 14th and continued through the evening. The winds toppled many trees and power lines. The fallen power lines led to thousands of custormers loosing power across the region at various times during the event. The combination of sparks from fallen power lines and dry conditions contributed to several small grass fires. Minor structural damage was also reported with some roof damage. A roof was partially torn from a gas station in Ashland in northeast Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph toppled numerous trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late afternoon and evening of the 31st in advance of a cold front and upper disturbance crossing the region. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...large hail and several tornados.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado in the vicinity of Easton. The tornado touched down near the Egypt Road and was intermittently on the ground while tracking northeast across U.S. Route 1A south of Easton before dissipating just before crossing the border into New Brunswick. Along the tornado path...hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted in intermittent swaths and patches (DI 27, DOD 4). Several outbuildings were also damaged or destroyed. The metal roof of a dairy barn was destroyed in Easton. Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated at around 95 mph. The average path width was around 50 yards. This same thunderstorm had produced another tornado several minutes earlier in the vicinity of Westfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late afternoon and evening of the 31st in advance of a cold front and upper disturbance crossing the region. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...large hail and several tornados.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced an EF0 tornado southeast of the town of Oxbow. The tornado began near the Oak Hill Lookout then was intermittently on the ground while travelling northeast across State Route 11 before dissipating. Along the tornado path...dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted in intermittent swaths and patches (DI 27, DOD 3). Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated at around 80 mph. The average path width was around 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 24th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...large hail and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado at the town of Eagle Lake. The tornado began near the Pinette Brook crossing of Convent Road. The tornado was then intermittently on the ground while moving through  Eagle Lake...eventually dissipating just before crossing State Route 11. Along the tornado path...hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted in intermittent swaths and patches (DI 27, DOD 4). The tornado also destroyed an outbuilding...moderately damaged one home and caused minor damage to several homes and vehicles. A transport trailer was also overturned. Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated at around 110 mph. The average path width was around 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River at Washburn caused brief flooding. The ice jam persisted for just over an hour before releasing. River levels quickly fell below flood stage with the release of the ice jam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River at Washburn caused brief flooding. River levels rose quickly behind the ice jam...cresting around 2.5 feet above flood stage. The ice jam persisted for just over an hour before releasing. River levels quickly fell with the release of the ice jam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of ice jams...rain and run-off from melting snow  contributed to flooding across Aroostook county. Many rivers and streams overflowed their banks...producing various degrees of flooding across the county. The flooding affected several towns and led to some road closures. Most of the flooding was minor...generally affecting lowland areas bordering the rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of ice jams...rain and run-off from melting snow contributed to flooding across Aroostook county. Many rivers and streams overflowed their banks...producing various degrees of flooding across the county. The flooding affected several towns and led to some road closures. Most of the flooding was minor...generally affecting lowland areas bordering the rivers and streams.|In the Fort Fairfield area an ice jam developed during the morning of the 6th in the vicinity of the bridge crossing the Aroostook River. The ice jam eventually increased to 3 miles in length. River levels rose quickly in the vicinity of the ice jam. Riverside Avenue was closed due to flooding...with one home evacuated near the river. The ice moved toward Tinker Dam that afternoon then re-jammed causing fluctuating river levels. The ice jam persisted near Tinker Dam through much of the 7th keeping river levels elevated. The ice jam released during the evening of the 7th...with river levels dropping rapidly with the release of the ice jam.  |A persistent ice jam on the Aroostook River between Washburn and Crouseville caused elevated river levels which flooded portions of Route 164 to depths of 1 to 2 feet in spots keeping the road closed for 4 days. The road was finally re-opened during the evening of the 10th.|An ice jam developed on the Saint John River near Keegan during the 6th and persisted through the 8th while slowly moving through Van Buren. The ice jam produced elevated river levels with some minor flooding. The flooding mostly involved spillover of water on to lowland areas and fields along the river.|Flooding across the rest of Aroostook county was generally minor...with mostly spillover on to lowlands and fields bordering rivers and streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of ice jams...rain and run-off from melting snow led to minor flooding across Penobscot county. Flooding across the county was generally minor and mostly involved spillover on to lowland areas and fields bordering rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of rain and run-off from melting snow led to minor flooding across Penobscot county. The flooding was generally minor and mostly involved spillover of water on to lowlands and fields bordering rivers and streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saint John River at Nine Mile Bridge caused minor flooding. The river crested around a foot above flood stage. River levels then quickly fell with the release of the ice jam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saint John River at Nine Mile Bridge caused minor flooding. The river crested around a foot above flood stage. The flooding affected wilderness areas of the North Maine Woods bordering the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Big Black River at Depot Mountain caused minor flooding. The river rose to around flood stage at which point river levels stabilized. The flooding affected wilderness areas of the North Maine Woods bordering the river. A second ice jam caused similar brief minor flooding with river levels rising to around flood stage for an hour before receding with the release of the ice jam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Big Black River at Depot Mountain caused minor flooding. The river rose to flood stage then stabilized. The river held around flood stage until the ice jam released. The flooding affected wilderness areas of the North Maine Woods bordering the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the Great Lakes on June 18th, passing through southern New England on the morning of June 19th. This resulted in a band of heavy rain over southern Maine. Rainfall amounts were around 3 inches which caused minor flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook (flood stage 15.0 ft) which crested at 17.4 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms containing heavy rain developed across Piscataquis county during the late evening of the 6th. Thunderstorms with rainfall rates of 1.0 to 1.5 inches per hour created localized flash flooding. The flash flooding washed out portions of several roads and driveways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Swift River at Roxbury (flood stage 7.0 ft) which crested at 8.1 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extratropical remnants of what had been Tropical Storm Danny brought high winds to southern Washington county while tracking along the Maine coast. The strong winds toppled trees in the towns of Cooper...Wesley...Charlotte and Eastport. Several boats were also torn from their moorings in the harbor at Eastport. The storm also brought heavy rains which led to road flooding in Bar Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire that damaged a garage in Sanford.  The lightning struck a tree that was a few feet from the garage and jumped to the corner of the garage roof.  The fire was contained with minimal damage to the garage and its contents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Bowdoinham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed a brief EF0 tornado damaged trees and a cornfield in East Bethel on the evening of July 18th. Maximum winds were estimated at 80 mph with a path length of 1.3 miles and maximum width of 100 yards. Damage was minor and limited to downed trees and a small portion of a corn field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado touched down in East Bethel downing trees and damaging a corn field. A National Weather Service storm survey team estimated winds at 80 mph. The path length was 1.3 miles with a maximum width of 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from thunderstorms that moved through coastal sections of Maine was responsible for a number of fires in York and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged four homes in the Kennebunk area.  In one incident, lightning struck a tall pine and followed the roots to underground pipes, and then followed the pipes to three separate homes.  In one of the homes, the doors and windows were blown out and the basement filled with smoke.  The two other homes lost appliances and electronic equipment.  In another incident, lightning struck a fourth home, but the damage was minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from thunderstorms that moved through coastal sections of Maine was responsible for a number of fires in York and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree and followed the ground to several homes in the immediate area.  The lightning left a large hole where it entered the ground.  Appliances and electronic equipment were damaged or destroyed by the lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from thunderstorms that moved through coastal sections of Maine was responsible for a number of fires in York and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Nobleboro and ignited a fire that destroyed the home.  A homeowner was home at the time and reported sparks throughout the kitchen area.  The fire spread rapidly throughout the structure and destroyed the home.  Damage was estimated at 1.5 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced several reports of small hail in York County during the late afternoon and evening on July 3rd. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced .75 hail near Limington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail near Limington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak cold front and short wave approaching from the Great Lakes. Heating in the morning provided a marginally unstable environment. Shear was expected to be relatively weak, and low wet bulb zero values suggested marginally severe hail was possible. Several severe thunderstorms did produce .75 hail along with one report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a gust to 59 mph measured by an ASOS at the Fryeburg Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 25-year-old man was struck by lightning as he stood under an overhang of a building to stay dry from a storm.  Both of his sneakers had holes burned in them.  The man was transported to a nearby medical facility for evaluation and then released.

Thunderstorms containing damaging winds and heavy rains occurred across Downeast Maine during the early morning hours of the 3rd. Rainfall rates of 2.0 to 2.5 inches per hour occurred with some of the thunderstorms leading to some road closures due to flash flooding. The flash flooding also damaged several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled on to power lines across the town of Bar Harbor by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. This led to widespread power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large waves generated from Hurricane Bill...which was tracking well east of the region...created high surf along the Maine coast. A large wave at Thunder Hole...in Acadia National Park...washed a group of spectators into the ocean. The spectators had gathered to watch the high surf break along the rocky coast. The wave washed 7 individuals into the ocean...4 of which were able to exit the water on their own. Of the remaining 3 individuals...2 were rescued by the Coast Guard. The final individual...a young girl...perished before she could be rescued. Other individuals not washed into the ocean were also injured when the same wave slammed them on to rocks along the shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf generated from Hurricane Bill...which was tracking well east of the region...produced large waves along the Maine coast. A large wave washed a group of spectators at Thunder Hole...in Acadia National Park...into the ocean. The spectators had gathered to watch the high surf break along the rocky coast. The wave washed 7 individuals into the water...4 of which were able to exit the water on their own. Of the remaining 3...2 were rescued by the Coast Guard. The final individual...a 7 year old girl...perished before she could be rescued. The girl was in the water for over 3 hours before her body was recovered. In addition to the fatality...13 individuals were injured when the same wave slammed people on to rocks. The injuries varied in severity...ranging from broken and dislocated bones to lacerations. Some of the injured had to be transported to the hospital...while others transported themselves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire in a three-story barn that was used for storing crafts, artwork, and antiques.  Volatile chemicals in the barn helped fuel the fire that destroyed the barn and its contents.

A squall line developed in warm sector characterized by moderate low-level shear and low LCLs. Wind damage was the most common form of severe weather, however a few cells showed extremely high cores and low-level rotation producing large hail and numerous reports of heavy rain. There were also several reports of funnel clouds and 1 confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on power lines in Solon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the west side of Norway Lake in Norway and moved east-northeast for more than 16 miles through the towns of Paris, Buckfield and Sumner before dissipating in the town of Hartford.  The tornado produced an intermittent path of damage along it's route.  Thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted by the tornado.  In addition...numerous buildings were damaged or destroyed by the |tornado's winds or by falling trees.  Also...many wires and utility poles were brought down by the falling trees.||The tornado was rated as EF-1 on the Enhanced Fugita Scale with maximum winds estimated between 100 and 110 mph and an estimated maximum damage path width of 700 yards (0.4 miles).||Tornado damage was observed near or across the following roads:||In Norway...Mallard Way, Dove Way, Crockett Ridge Road, Pleasant Road, Ralph Richardson Road.||In Paris...Durgin Road, Elm Hill Road, Military Road, High Street, Route 26 (Park Street), Utility Road, Paris Hill Road, Ryerson Road, and Dean Road.||In Buckfield...Allen School Road, Darnit Road, and River Road.||In Sumner...Bonney Road.||In Hartford...Rt 140 (Sumner Road), Carey Hill Road, Gurney Hill Road, Bear Pond Road (Rt 219).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Pleasant Street in Poland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in warm sector characterized by moderate low-level shear and low LCLs. Wind damage was the most common form of severe weather, however a few cells showed extremely high cores and low-level rotation producing large hail and numerous reports of heavy rain. There were also several reports of funnel clouds and 1 confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Gardiner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in warm sector characterized by moderate low-level shear and low LCLs. Wind damage was the most common form of severe weather, however a few cells showed extremely high cores and low-level rotation producing large hail and numerous reports of heavy rain. There were also several reports of funnel clouds and 1 confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on power lines in Solon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary upper level low in combination with a weak surface front resulted in 2 to 5 inches of rain in a 72 hour period. This caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs) which crested at 37,900 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak cold front and short wave approaching from the Great Lakes. Heating in the morning provided a marginally unstable environment. Shear was expected to be relatively weak, and low wet bulb zero values suggested marginally severe hail was possible. Several severe thunderstorms did produce .75 hail along with one report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail just west of the University of New England campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Ohio River Valley and into New England spreading heavy rain over the region. Rainfall amounts and rainfall rates where high enough to cause serious flooding in midcoast Maine. Rainfall amounts in the damaged areas ranged from 3 to 4.5 inches in 12 hours. Damage to roads in the area was extensive with blown out culverts and washed out roadbeds. Some schools in Appleton, Camden, Hope, and Licolnville were closed because of the road closures. Damage in Knox, Waldo, and Lincoln Counties amounted to more than $850,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in serious flooding of small rivers and streams in several towns in Knox County. Damage for Knox County was estimated at $44,528 which included $26,528 in Camden, $8000 in Hope, $5,000 in Warren and $5,000 in Rockport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon of the 26th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The thunderstorms continued into the evening. The thunderstorms produced a weak tornado...large hail...damaging winds and flash flooding.

Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail occurred across northern Maine during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled on Central Park Tote Road in Baxter State Park by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.
